name: Krishna Yadav
role: Data Analyst | BI Developer | Web Application Builder
summary: I build business dashboards, data analysis workflows, and modern web applications using Python, SQL, Power BI, React, and Node.js.
headline: Hi, I'm Krishna. I build dashboards, data tools, and scalable web applications.
focusAreas: Exploratory data analysis and insight storytelling
Machine learning prototypes and model evaluation

education.degree: Master of Computer Applications, Data Science
Bachelor of Computer Applications, Computer Science

projects.title: Sales Dashboard in Power BI
projects.problem: Sales leaders needed a deployed dashboard experience to monitor revenue, profit, orders, regional performance, and KPI movement quickly.
projects.role: Designed the dashboard story, organized KPI sections, built the deployed analytics interface, and shaped the recruiter-facing live view.
projects.approach: Created a live sales dashboard with KPI cards, performance visuals, regional insights, and a clean layout for fast business review.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live dashboard experience that makes sales trends, weak regions, and target performance easier to scan.
projects.impact: Tracked 8 sales KPIs across region, category, revenue, profit, orders, growth, returns, and target achievement.

projects.title: HR CV Shortlister
projects.problem: Recruiters need a faster deployed tool to review resumes, compare candidate profiles, and shortlist relevant applicants.
projects.role: Built the shortlisting flow, structured candidate evaluation, and deployed the project for HR screening use cases.
projects.approach: Used automation-focused logic to collect candidate inputs, compare role fit, and support faster screening decisions.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live HR tech tool that reduces manual review effort and improves candidate filtering workflow.
projects.impact: Designed to reduce resume screening time by up to 40% by prioritizing role-fit signals before manual review.

projects.title: Real-Time Sales Analytics (PULSE)
projects.problem: Sales teams needed a live, streaming view of transactions instead of stale end-of-day reports to react to demand shifts as they happen.
projects.role: Designed the streaming pipeline architecture and built the deployed real-time dashboard interface.
projects.approach: Built a Kafka-based ingestion pipeline where POS and web events flow through a Python consumer for validation and enrichment, land in Postgres, and surface live in a Power BI-style DirectQuery dashboard.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live-updating dashboard showing revenue, orders, average order value, and conversion rate with a real-time transaction feed.
projects.impact: Modeled a full Kafka to Python to SQL to BI pipeline capable of processing streaming sales events with sub-15-second dashboard refresh.

projects.title: Financial Performance Dashboard (Ledger)
projects.problem: Finance teams needed a consolidated view of revenue, cost, and margin trends without manually reconciling spreadsheets each period.
projects.role: Built the dashboard's data model, KPI logic, and the deployed analytics interface, including a data upload flow for custom datasets.
projects.approach: Structured a SQL-based fact and dimension model feeding DAX measures for Net Margin % and YoY Growth, with support for connecting a CSV, Excel, Power BI export, or live API to recompute the dashboard.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live financial dashboard covering revenue vs. expenses vs. profit trends, expense mix, and a period-by-period ledger detail view.
projects.impact: Built reusable SQL and DAX logic (Net Margin %, YoY Growth) that recomputes automatically for any connected dataset matching the schema.

projects.title: Churn Radar Dashboard
projects.problem: Businesses struggle to spot at-risk customers early, leading to lost revenue from preventable churn.
projects.role: Designed the churn analysis workflow, built the deployed dashboard interface, and structured the risk-scoring visuals.
projects.approach: Built a live churn analytics dashboard that segments customers by risk level, surfaces key churn drivers, and visualizes retention trends for fast decision-making.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live dashboard that helps identify high-risk customer segments and prioritize retention efforts.
projects.impact: Enabled quick identification of at-risk customer segments through visual risk scoring and trend tracking.

projects.title: E-Commerce Sales Analytics
projects.problem: E-commerce leadership needed a single view across platforms, categories, and customer segments to spot where revenue growth wasn't reaching profit.
projects.role: Designed the multi-tab analytics structure and built the deployed dashboard covering overview, customers, products, platforms, and profitability.
projects.approach: Analyzed a synthetic dataset of 6,000 customers, 320 products, and 34,185 orders across platforms to build revenue, margin, LTV/CAC, delivery, and return-rate visuals.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live command-center dashboard revealing that marketplace commissions change profitability rankings even when a platform leads on raw revenue.
projects.impact: Surfaced actionable signals such as category-level return-rate drivers and weak-ROI acquisition channels across 34,185 orders.

projects.title: Corex Enterprises
projects.problem: Corex Enterprises needed a deployed business website with a modern first impression, service clarity, and responsive presentation.
projects.role: Created the frontend structure, organized business content, improved page sections, and prepared the site for Netlify deployment.
projects.approach: Built a clean React-based website flow with reusable sections, professional visual hierarchy, and mobile-friendly styling.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live business website that supports service discovery, brand trust, and future lead capture improvements.
projects.impact: Created a deployed company website with responsive sections, clear service discovery, and production-ready presentation.

projects.title: Self-Learning AI for Games
projects.problem: Demonstrate reinforcement-style learning by training an AI agent to play Flappy Bird without hard-coded gameplay rules.
projects.role: Built the Python simulation, integrated NEAT training, and visualized agent performance across generations.
projects.approach: Integrated NEAT-Python with Pygame and visualized training behavior using Python analysis libraries.
projects.outcome: Created an adaptive AI simulation that learns through neuroevolution and performance feedback.
projects.impact: Improved agent survival across training generations and visualized model learning behavior for clear evaluation.

projects.title: Quantum Error Correction Simulation
projects.problem: Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum systems require benchmarking of fidelity, error rates, and mitigation strategies.
projects.role: Implemented simulation workflows and compared QEC behavior across fidelity and error-rate benchmarks.
projects.approach: Simulated QEC protocols with IBM Qiskit and Google Cirq, comparing resource-efficient designs for chemistry and optimization use cases.
projects.outcome: Built benchmark logic around qubit overhead, decoherence, and hybrid QEC-mitigation strategies.
projects.impact: Compared fidelity and error-rate behavior across simulation runs to support more reliable experiment decisions.

certifications: Python for Data Science - IBM PY0101EN

blogPosts.title: How I approach a sales dashboard project
blogPosts.summary: A practical breakdown of how I clean data, choose KPIs, design visuals, and turn dashboard pages into business decisions.

content.text: Every sales dashboard I've built has followed roughly the same process, whether it's a single-page Excel summary or a multi-page Power BI report with drill-through. The tools change project to project, but the sequence of decisions doesn't. This is a full walkthrough of that process, from the first conversation with a stakeholder to the dashboard actually being used to make decisions.
Step 1: Start With the Decisions, Not the Data

The biggest mistake in dashboard work is opening the spreadsheet before asking what the dashboard is actually for. Before touching any data, I sit down with whoever requested the dashboard, usually a sales manager or business owner, and ask a short set of questions: What decision are you trying to make with this? How often will you look at it? What does 'good' look like this month versus last month? Who else will see it?

The answers usually narrow a vague request like 'I want a sales dashboard' into something concrete: 'I need to know, every Monday, which regions are behind target and which reps need a check-in call.' That single sentence already tells me the grain of the data (weekly, by region and by rep), the primary KPI (target vs. actual), and the audience (a sales manager doing a Monday review, not a finance team doing quarterly reporting).
Step 2: Audit the Data Before Promising Anything

Once I know what the dashboard needs to answer, I look at the actual source data, usually a CRM export, an ERP report, or a set of Excel files different regional teams maintain independently. This step exists to catch problems early: inconsistent product naming across regions, missing customer IDs, date fields stored as text, or duplicate entries from manual data entry.

I've learned to flag these issues to the stakeholder immediately rather than quietly working around them. If sales data from one region uses a different currency or a different fiscal calendar, that's a business decision to resolve, not something an analyst should silently normalize without checking. Surfacing data quality issues early also sets realistic expectations about timeline; a dashboard is only as reliable as the data feeding it.
Step 3: Clean and Model the Data

With the scope confirmed, the actual cleaning happens, usually in Power Query if the destination is Power BI or Excel, or in Pandas if the pipeline needs to run on a schedule. Common tasks at this stage: standardizing date formats, mapping inconsistent region or product names to a single lookup table, removing test transactions, and splitting combined fields like 'City, State' into separate columns.

Once cleaned, the data gets modeled. For anything beyond a single flat table, I build a simple star schema: one fact table (the sales transactions) connected to dimension tables (date, product, region, salesperson). This keeps the model fast and makes every calculation, from total revenue to year-over-year growth, consistent across every visual in the report instead of being recalculated differently on each page.
Step 4: Pick the KPIs Before Building a Single Chart

items: Revenue vs. target, the headline number almost every sales dashboard needs
Growth rate, usually month-over-month and year-over-year
Win rate or conversion rate, if a pipeline/funnel view is part of the ask
Top and bottom performers, by region, product line, or rep
Average deal size, which often reveals more than total revenue alone

content.text: I keep this list short on purpose. A dashboard with fifteen KPIs on the front page is one nobody actually reads; a dashboard with five clear numbers gets checked every week. Secondary metrics still matter, but they go on a second page or behind a drill-through, not competing for attention on the summary view.
Step 5: Wireframe Before Building

Before opening Power BI or Excel to actually build anything, I sketch the layout, sometimes literally on paper, sometimes in a simple slide. This forces a decision about hierarchy: what's the first thing a viewer's eye should land on, what's a supporting detail, and what filters (date range, region, product) need to be available without cluttering the view. Getting stakeholder sign-off on a five-minute wireframe saves hours of rebuilding later.
Step 6: Build, Starting With the Model, Not the Visuals

The build itself starts with the data model and core DAX measures (CALCULATE, SUMX, time-intelligence functions like SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR), confirmed against a few known numbers the stakeholder can sanity-check manually. Only once the underlying math is verified do the actual charts get built: a trend line for revenue over time, a bar chart for regional comparison, KPI cards for the headline numbers, and slicers for date range and region.

Design choices at this stage stay deliberately restrained: two or three colors used consistently (not a different color per chart), aligned grids, and enough white space that the dashboard is scannable in under ten seconds. Anything that requires a legend to decode a color scheme usually gets simplified.
Step 7: Test With Real Numbers the Stakeholder Already Knows

Before calling it done, I walk through the dashboard with the stakeholder using a month they already know the answer for. If they know last March's revenue was a specific number and the dashboard shows something different, that's caught before go-live, not three months later when a decision gets made off a wrong number.
Step 8: Deploy and Set Up Refresh

For Power BI, this means publishing to the Power BI Service and setting a scheduled refresh against the live data source, so the dashboard updates automatically instead of requiring someone to manually re-run a report. For Excel-based dashboards, it means making sure the data connections point to a shared, stable file location rather than someone's local desktop, so it keeps working after the person who built it moves on to something else.
What Actually Makes the Difference

None of these steps are complicated individually. The value is in the order, understanding the decision before touching data, auditing before cleaning, modeling before visualizing, and testing against known numbers before deployment. Skipping straight to 'build the charts' is how dashboards end up technically impressive but practically ignored a month later.

blogPosts.title: Python libraries I use for analytics
blogPosts.summary: Notes on Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Scikit-learn for analysis, visualization, and machine learning prototypes.

content.text: Python's data ecosystem has a lot of libraries, and it's easy to get lost trying to learn all of them at once. In practice, a handful cover almost every analytics task I run into: Pandas and NumPy for handling and computing on data, Matplotlib and Seaborn for visualizing it, and Scikit-learn for the occasional predictive model. Here's how each one fits into a real analysis workflow, not just what each one technically does.
Pandas: The Foundation of Almost Everything

Pandas is where nearly every analysis starts. Its core object, the DataFrame, is essentially a spreadsheet inside Python, rows and columns, but with far more power to filter, transform, and combine data than a spreadsheet formula bar allows. Reading a CSV, Excel file, or SQL query result into a DataFrame with one line (pd.read_csv, pd.read_excel, pd.read_sql) is usually the first step of any project.

From there, the day-to-day work is mostly a combination of filtering rows (df[df['revenue'] > 1000]), grouping and aggregating (df.groupby('region')['revenue'].sum()), merging tables the way you'd do a SQL JOIN (pd.merge), and handling missing data (df.dropna() or df.fillna()). The groupby-aggregate pattern in particular shows up constantly, it's the same logic as a PivotTable in Excel or GROUP BY in SQL, just expressed in code, which makes it repeatable and easy to rerun on new data without redoing manual steps.

Pandas also handles dates well, once a column is converted with pd.to_datetime, extracting the month, quarter, or day-of-week for trend analysis becomes a single attribute call instead of a manual formula. For anyone coming from Excel, the mental model translates directly: Pandas does the same things, just at scale and with full reproducibility.
NumPy: The Engine Underneath

NumPy rarely gets used directly for day-to-day analysis, but it's the engine Pandas is built on, and it matters for anything involving numerical computation at scale: array operations, statistical functions (mean, standard deviation, percentiles), and linear algebra if a project needs it. The main practical benefit is speed. Vectorized NumPy operations (working on an entire array at once) run dramatically faster than looping through rows one at a time in plain Python, which matters once a dataset grows past a few thousand rows.

In practice, NumPy shows up most when generating synthetic or randomized data for testing, doing quick statistical calculations outside a full DataFrame, or when a Pandas operation under the hood needs a NumPy array format to run efficiently.
Matplotlib: Full Control, More Typing

Matplotlib is the foundational plotting library nearly everything else in the Python visualization ecosystem builds on. It's verbose, building even a simple chart takes several lines to set labels, titles, and figure size, but that verbosity comes with complete control. When a chart needs a very specific layout, custom annotations, or has to match an exact style for a report, Matplotlib is what makes that possible.

For quick exploratory charts during analysis, I'll usually reach for Pandas' built-in .plot() method (which uses Matplotlib underneath) since it's a one-liner off a DataFrame. Matplotlib itself comes out when the chart needs to be presentation-ready: consistent fonts, precise annotations, or multiple subplots arranged in a specific grid.
Seaborn: Matplotlib, But Statistical and Faster

Seaborn sits on top of Matplotlib and is built specifically for statistical visualization; it understands DataFrames natively and produces cleaner default styling with far less code. A correlation heatmap, a distribution plot, or a boxplot comparing categories across a variable takes one function call in Seaborn versus significantly more manual setup in raw Matplotlib.

This is usually the first place I look during exploratory data analysis (EDA): a quick sns.histplot() to see a variable's distribution, sns.boxplot() to spot outliers across categories, or sns.heatmap() on a correlation matrix to see which numeric fields move together before deciding what's worth investigating further.
Scikit-learn: For When Analysis Becomes Prediction

Most analyst-level work is descriptive: what happened, and why. Scikit-learn comes in when a project crosses into predictive territory, forecasting next quarter's churn risk, scoring leads, or clustering customers into segments. It provides a consistent interface across dozens of algorithms (linear regression, random forests, k-means clustering), so once you understand the fit/predict pattern for one model, switching models later is mostly a one-line change.

For a typical portfolio-level project, this usually means: splitting data into training and test sets (train_test_split), fitting a model, and evaluating it with an appropriate metric (accuracy or F1 score for classification, RMSE for regression). The value isn't in using the most sophisticated algorithm, it's in correctly framing the problem, preparing clean features, and being honest about how well the model actually performs on unseen data.
How These Fit Together in a Real Project

A typical end-to-end task looks like this: load raw data with Pandas, clean and reshape it, use NumPy under the hood for any numeric transformations, explore distributions and relationships with Seaborn, build any predictive component with Scikit-learn if needed, and finish with Matplotlib for the final, presentation-ready chart that goes into a report or a stakeholder deck. None of these tools replace each other, they cover different stages of the same pipeline, and knowing which one to reach for at each stage is most of what makes the workflow efficient.
A Realistic Example: Cleaning and Exploring a Sales Export

Say the raw input is a monthly sales export with inconsistent date formats, a few duplicate rows from re-exported data, and some missing customer IDs. The first pass in Pandas usually looks like: read the file, check df.info() and df.isnull().sum() to see what's missing, drop exact duplicates with df.drop_duplicates(), and convert the date column with pd.to_datetime(). From there, df.groupby(df['date'].dt.month)['revenue'].sum() gives a fast monthly revenue trend without writing a single formula by hand.

Once the data is clean, a quick Seaborn sns.lineplot() shows the trend visually, and a sns.boxplot() grouped by region surfaces whether one region has unusually high variance compared to the rest, often the first sign something in that region's data (or business process) is different from the others. This exploratory pass, usually ten to fifteen minutes of code, tends to reveal more about a dataset than an hour of scrolling through it in a spreadsheet.
Why Learn Pandas Before Anything Else

For anyone starting out, the ROI on learning Pandas thoroughly before moving to Seaborn or Scikit-learn is high, since Pandas is the layer every other tool sits on top of. A visualization or a model is only as good as the DataFrame feeding it, and most real analysis time goes into getting that DataFrame clean and correctly shaped, not into the final chart or model itself.

blogPosts.title: What makes a good analyst portfolio
blogPosts.summary: A portfolio should show business context, tools used, measurable outcomes, and clean storytelling instead of only screenshots.

content.text: Most data analyst portfolios look the same: a Power BI dashboard screenshot, a one-line description, a GitHub link that may or may not still run. After reviewing and building a fair number of these, the portfolios that actually get someone an interview share a specific set of traits, and it has less to do with the tools used and more to do with how the work is presented.
Business Context Comes Before the Chart

The single biggest difference between a portfolio that gets attention and one that doesn't is whether each project opens with a business problem or with a chart. 'Built a dashboard using Power BI and DAX' tells a recruiter nothing about judgment or impact. 'Sales leadership couldn't tell which regions were underperforming until the end of the quarter, so I built a dashboard that flags at-risk regions weekly' tells them exactly what problem was being solved and why it mattered.

This framing matters because most people reviewing a portfolio, whether a recruiter or a hiring manager, are not going to dig through your code or click every filter on your dashboard. They're scanning for evidence that you understand how analytics work connects to real decisions. A one-paragraph problem statement at the top of each project does more of that work than the dashboard itself.
Fewer Projects, Shown Properly, Beat a Long List

A portfolio with eight half-finished projects reads as less credible than one with three that are clearly thought through end-to-end. For most early-career analyst roles, two or three strong projects that each cover a different skill (a BI dashboard, a Python-based analysis, a SQL-heavy data pipeline) demonstrate range without diluting attention. Padding the list with rushed side-projects usually backfires, since the first thing an interviewer often does is click into whichever project looks weakest.
Every Project Needs a Measurable Outcome

items: Not just 'analyzed sales data' but 'identified a 12% revenue gap concentrated in two regions'
Not just 'built a churn model' but 'flagged the top 15% highest-risk customers with 78% precision'
Not just 'created a dashboard' but 'reduced weekly reporting time from three hours to fifteen minutes'

content.text: These numbers don't need to come from a real client engagement to be credible, portfolio projects built on public datasets can still state a specific, calculated outcome. What matters is that the outcome is concrete and measurable rather than a vague claim like 'provided valuable insights,' which signals nothing about the actual result.
Show the Tools, But Don't Lead With Them

Listing every tool used (Power BI, SQL, Python, DAX, Excel) still matters, recruiters and applicant tracking systems do scan for these keywords. The mistake is putting the tool list first and the business problem second. A good structure per project is: the problem, the approach (mentioning the tools naturally as part of the story), and the result. This way the tool list is present without becoming the entire pitch.
A Working Live Link Matters More Than Perfect Code

A dashboard that a recruiter can actually click into and interact with, live, in their browser, is worth more than a beautifully commented GitHub repo they'll never clone. If a project's centerpiece is a dashboard, host it somewhere accessible (Power BI public publish, a deployed web app, or an exported PDF at minimum) rather than assuming someone will download and open a file locally. If the project is code-heavy instead, a clean README with a clear problem statement matters more than exhaustive comments.
Case Study Pages Beat Screenshot Galleries

A dedicated page per project, with a written breakdown of problem, role, approach, and outcome, gives both recruiters and search engines something substantial to engage with. A portfolio that's just a grid of screenshots with one-line captions reads as unfinished, even if the underlying work was thorough. The extra half hour spent writing a proper case study per project usually does more for the portfolio's credibility than another day spent polishing the dashboard's colors.
Common Mistakes Worth Avoiding

items: Screenshots with no explanation of what decision the dashboard supports
Generic project titles like 'Dashboard 1' instead of naming the business problem
No mention of data cleaning or modeling decisions, as if the data arrived perfectly ready to visualize
Overloading the homepage with every tool ever touched, instead of the two or three used most seriously
No easy way to actually see the finished work without downloading a file first

content.text: The Portfolio Is a Sales Document, Not a Diary

It's easy to think of a portfolio as documentation of everything you've learned. It works better when treated as a short pitch: here's a real problem, here's how I approached it, here's the measurable result, here's how to reach me. Everything that doesn't serve that pitch, extra tools, unfinished side-projects, overly technical jargon, is worth cutting even if it was interesting to build.
What Recruiters Actually Spend Time On

In practice, most recruiters spend under a minute on an initial portfolio scan. That minute is usually split between the headline summary at the top, the titles of two or three projects, and whichever one project looks most relevant to the role they're hiring for. This is why the project titles themselves matter more than most people expect, 'Regional Churn Risk Dashboard' gets more attention in that first scan than 'Project 3'.

If the portfolio survives that first minute, the next few minutes usually go to one project in depth, reading through problem, approach, and outcome, before deciding whether to reach out. That's the moment a well-written case study page earns its keep; a project that only exists as a screenshot has nothing left to hold attention past the first scan.
How Much Detail Is Too Much

There's a balance between a project page that's too thin (a screenshot and one sentence) and one that's exhaustingly long (a full technical writeup better suited to a blog post than a portfolio page). A good target per project case study is roughly 150-300 words covering problem, role, approach, and result, enough to demonstrate thinking without requiring a ten-minute read. If a project genuinely needs more explanation than that, a linked full write-up or blog post is a better home for the extra depth than cramming it all into the portfolio page itself.

blogPosts.title: How to Build an Excel Dashboard From Scratch (Step-by-Step Guide)
blogPosts.summary: A practical walkthrough for turning raw spreadsheet data into a clean, interactive Excel dashboard with KPIs, charts, and slicers.

content.text: An Excel dashboard is one of the fastest ways to turn a messy spreadsheet into something a manager can actually use to make decisions. This guide walks through the exact process I follow when building one, from raw data to a finished, interactive dashboard, including the specific formulas and settings that make the difference between a dashboard people actually use and one that gets opened once and forgotten.
Step 1: Get the Data Into a Proper Table

Before any chart gets built, the raw data needs to sit inside an Excel Table (select the range, then Insert > Table, or Ctrl+T). Tables auto-expand as new rows are added, keep formulas consistent down every row automatically, and make every downstream step easier, especially PivotTables and slicers, which work far more reliably against a named Table than a plain cell range.

Give the table a clear name (Table Design > Table Name) instead of leaving it as the default 'Table1'. This matters once a workbook has several tables feeding different parts of the dashboard, formulas referencing 'SalesData[Revenue]' are far easier to audit later than formulas referencing 'Sheet1!$C$2:$C$4501'.
Step 2: Clean the Data With Power Query

Use Power Query (Data tab > Get & Transform > From Table/Range) to remove duplicates, fix inconsistent date formats, trim whitespace, and split combined columns. Common cleaning steps: Remove Duplicates, Change Column Type (forcing text-formatted numbers back into actual numbers), Trim (removing leading/trailing spaces that silently break VLOOKUP matches), and Split Column by Delimiter for fields like 'City, State' that arrive combined.

The advantage of doing this in Power Query rather than manually is that every step gets recorded as a reusable query. When next month's data arrives in the same raw format, right-clicking the query and choosing Refresh reruns the entire cleanup automatically, instead of repeating the same manual edits by hand every reporting cycle.
Step 3: Decide the KPIs Before Building Any Chart

The biggest mistake in dashboard building is starting with charts instead of questions. Pick four to eight KPIs that actually matter to the business, revenue, profit margin, order volume, top region, or customer churn are common examples, and design every visual around answering those questions clearly. Write these KPIs down on paper before opening Excel; it keeps the dashboard focused instead of growing to include every metric that could theoretically be calculated.
Step 4: Build the Core Visuals

items: PivotTables and PivotCharts for summarizing large datasets quickly (Insert > PivotTable, then drag fields into Rows, Columns, and Values)
Line charts for trends over time (monthly revenue, order growth)
Bar charts for comparisons across categories (region, product, team)
Card-style cells (large bold numbers, often built with a merged cell and a big font size) for single headline KPIs
Conditional formatting or heatmaps (Home > Conditional Formatting > Color Scales) to highlight outliers at a glance

content.text: A PivotChart connected to a PivotTable is usually the fastest way to get an interactive chart without writing any formulas, changing the underlying data automatically updates the chart on refresh. For headline KPI numbers, a simple formula like =SUM(SalesData[Revenue]) referencing the Table directly (rather than a fixed cell range) keeps the number accurate even as rows are added.
Step 5: Add Interactivity With Slicers and Dropdowns

Slicers (Insert > Slicer, available once you have a PivotTable) connected to PivotTables let anyone viewing the dashboard filter by region, date, or category without touching a formula, just clicking a button. Multiple PivotTables and PivotCharts can be connected to the same slicer through Slicer > Report Connections, so one click filters the entire dashboard at once instead of each chart needing its own filter.

Data validation dropdowns (Data > Data Validation > List) paired with XLOOKUP (=XLOOKUP(selected_value, SalesData[Region], SalesData[Revenue])) can drive dynamic summary views for non-technical users, letting someone pick a region from a dropdown and see that region's numbers update instantly in a summary cell, without needing to understand PivotTables at all.
Step 6: Design for Clarity, Not Decoration

Keep the color palette to two or three consistent colors, align everything to a grid (View > Gridlines can be turned off for the final look, but keep elements visually aligned during layout), and remove unnecessary borders and default gridlines. A dashboard should be scannable in under ten seconds, if a viewer has to hunt for the number that matters, the layout needs simplifying, not more decoration.

A useful habit: build the dashboard on its own sheet, separate from the raw data and any intermediate calculation sheets. Hide the data and calculation sheets (right-click the sheet tab > Hide) before sharing the file, so the person opening it sees only the polished dashboard view, not the working scratch area behind it.
Step 7: Protect and Share the Final File

Once the dashboard is finished, protecting the sheet (Review > Protect Sheet) prevents accidental edits to formulas while still allowing slicers and dropdowns to function. If the dashboard needs to update automatically from a shared data source, storing the workbook in SharePoint or OneDrive and using Power Query's 'Refresh on Open' setting (Data > Queries & Connections > right-click the query > Properties) means the dashboard shows current numbers every time it's opened, without anyone needing to manually re-run anything.
Common Mistakes to Avoid

items: Hardcoding values instead of linking to the data table, which silently breaks the dashboard the next time data updates
Too many colors or 3D chart effects that reduce readability rather than adding insight
Mixing raw data sheets with the dashboard view instead of separating and hiding them
Skipping data validation, so a single bad row (a text value in a numeric column, for example) breaks every chart downstream
Building charts before deciding what question each one answers, leading to a dashboard full of charts nobody asked for

content.text: A Note on Print and PDF Export

If the dashboard will ever be printed or shared as a PDF (common for executive summaries), set the print area explicitly (Page Layout > Print Area > Set Print Area) and check Page Layout view before sharing, dashboards designed for a wide screen often overflow badly onto a portrait-oriented printed page. Setting the page to landscape orientation and using 'Fit to 1 page wide' under Page Setup usually resolves this.
Where This Leads Next

Once this structure is solid, clean data feeding a table, clear KPIs, purposeful charts, working filters, this same workflow translates directly into tools like Power BI, which handles larger datasets and more complex data models more gracefully than Excel, and is usually the next step once an analyst's Excel dashboards start hitting the size or complexity where PivotTables and formulas become difficult to maintain.

blogPosts.title: Power BI for Beginners: A Practical Starting Guide
blogPosts.summary: Everything a first-time Power BI user needs to go from installing the tool to publishing an interactive report: data loading, modeling, DAX basics, and more.

content.text: Power BI is Microsoft's business intelligence tool for turning raw data into interactive reports and dashboards. This guide covers the core workflow every beginner needs to understand before building their first real report, going deep enough on data modeling and DAX that the concepts actually stick, not just a list of menu clicks.
1. Install Power BI Desktop and Understand the Two Halves of the Tool

Power BI Desktop is free and available directly from Microsoft. It's where reports get built; the separate Power BI Service (app.powerbi.com) is where finished reports get published and shared with a team. Beginners often confuse the two, Desktop is for building, Service is for distributing and scheduling refreshes. Nearly everything covered here happens in Desktop, with Service coming in only at the final publishing step.
2. Connect to a Data Source

Use Get Data (Home tab) to connect to Excel files, CSVs, SQL Server, or web APIs. For a first project, an Excel sales dataset is the easiest starting point since the structure is already familiar. Power BI also supports connecting directly to a live SQL database, which matters once a report needs to refresh against a source that updates continuously rather than a static file.
3. Clean Data in Power Query Editor

Every connected table opens in Power Query Editor first (accessible via Transform Data). This is where column types get fixed, blank rows get removed, and columns get renamed before anything touches a chart. Treat this step as non-negotiable, dashboards built on unclean data are unreliable no matter how good the visuals look. Common transformations: Remove Duplicates, Change Type (especially fixing dates that import as text), Split Column, and Merge Queries (Power BI's equivalent of a SQL JOIN, combining two tables based on a matching column).

Every transformation applied here gets recorded as a step in the Applied Steps panel, meaning the entire cleanup process reruns automatically the next time the underlying data refreshes. This is one of Power BI's biggest advantages over manually cleaning data in Excel every reporting period.
4. Build the Data Model (Relationships)

Real projects usually involve multiple tables, sales, products, regions, dates. In the Model view, connect these tables using relationships (usually one-to-many) so a single slicer can filter every visual across the report. A clean star schema, one central fact table (transactions) connected to smaller dimension tables (date, product, region), keeps performance fast and DAX formulas simple.

A dedicated Date table deserves special mention. Power BI can auto-generate one, but building a proper Date table manually (or via a DAX CALENDAR() function) unlocks time-intelligence functions like SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR and TOTALYTD, which are difficult to write correctly without a continuous, marked date dimension in the model.
5. Learn Basic DAX
DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language behind every calculated metric in Power BI. A handful of functions cover most beginner needs:

items: SUM() and AVERAGE() for basic aggregations, the DAX equivalent of an Excel SUM formula
CALCULATE() to apply filters to an aggregation, the single most important DAX function to learn early, since almost every advanced measure is built around it
SUMX() for row-by-row calculations before summing, useful for metrics like revenue = price × quantity where the calculation has to happen per row before totaling
DIVIDE() instead of a plain division operator, since it safely handles division-by-zero without breaking the report
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR() and TOTALYTD() for year-over-year and year-to-date comparisons, once a proper Date table is in place

content.text: A useful mental model for CALCULATE: it takes a base calculation (like SUM of revenue) and lets you override the filter context around it, for example, calculating revenue for just the current year regardless of what date range a slicer is set to. Most 'advanced' DAX measures are really just CALCULATE with a specific filter condition layered on top.
6. Build the First Visuals

Start simple: a bar chart for category comparison, a line chart for trend over time, and a few KPI cards for headline numbers. Add slicers for date range and category so the report becomes interactive rather than a static image. Power BI's Format pane (the paint-roller icon) controls every visual detail, colors, data labels, axis formatting, so it's worth spending time here rather than accepting default styling, which rarely matches a company's actual branding or the report's intended tone.
7. Use Bookmarks and Drill-Through for Interactivity

Once the basics are solid, Bookmarks (View tab) let you save a specific state of filters and visual selections, useful for building guided 'story' navigation through a report. Drill-through pages let a viewer right-click a data point (like a specific region on a bar chart) and jump to a detail page filtered to just that region, without needing separate reports for summary and detail views.
8. Publish and Share

Once the report looks right, use Publish (Home tab) to push it to the Power BI Service, where it can be shared as a link, embedded in a webpage, or scheduled to refresh automatically from the original data source (Settings > Scheduled Refresh, available for cloud-connected sources like a hosted database, or via a Gateway installation for on-premises files).
A Note on Row-Level Security

For reports shared across a team where different people should only see their own region or department's data, Row-Level Security (Modeling tab > Manage Roles) restricts what each viewer sees within the same published report, rather than needing a separate report copy per team. This is worth knowing exists even if a first project doesn't need it, since it comes up quickly once a report moves from a personal project to a shared team tool.
Where to Go Next

After the basics, the next skills worth learning are DAX time-intelligence functions in more depth, calculated columns versus measures (a common early point of confusion, calculated columns are computed row-by-row and stored, measures are computed on the fly based on filter context), Power BI Service workspaces for team collaboration, and performance optimization once a model grows past a few hundred thousand rows, mainly around reducing calculated columns in favor of measures and keeping the data model in a proper star schema rather than one wide flat table.

blogPosts.title: 20 SQL Interview Questions Every Data Analyst Should Know
blogPosts.summary: A concise set of SQL interview questions and answers covering joins, aggregations, subqueries, and window functions, organized from basic to advanced.

content.text: These are the SQL questions that come up most often in data analyst interviews, grouped by difficulty. Understanding the reasoning behind each answer matters more than memorizing the syntax, interviewers usually probe follow-up questions to see if you actually understand why one approach is better than another, not just whether you can recite the right keyword.
Basics

items: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING? WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after a GROUP BY / aggregation has been applied. A common mistake is trying to filter on an aggregate function (like COUNT(*) > 5) using WHERE, which fails because the aggregation hasn't happened yet at that point in query execution.

What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP? DELETE removes rows and can be rolled back (and can use a WHERE clause); TRUNCATE removes all rows quickly and resets identity but can't be filtered and is harder to roll back; DROP removes the entire table structure permanently.

What is a primary key vs a foreign key? A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table and cannot be NULL; a foreign key references a primary key in another table to enforce a relationship between the two.
What does DISTINCT do? It removes duplicate rows from the result set, based on all selected columns together, not just one column at a time unless only that column is selected.

What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL? UNION removes duplicate rows across the combined result sets, which requires an extra sort/comparison step; UNION ALL keeps every row, including duplicates, and is faster since it skips the duplicate check.

content.text: Joins and Grouping

items: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN? INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right, filling unmatched columns with NULL. This distinction matters constantly in reporting, a LEFT JOIN from 'all customers' to 'orders' correctly shows customers with zero orders, an INNER JOIN would silently drop them.

What does a self join do? It joins a table to itself, typically used for hierarchical data like an employee table referencing a manager's ID stored in the same table, letting you pull an employee's name alongside their manager's name in one row.
How do you find duplicate rows in a table? Group by the columns that define a duplicate and use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1, which returns the specific combinations that appear more than once.

What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY? GROUP BY aggregates rows into summary groups (collapsing multiple rows into one per group); ORDER BY simply sorts the final result set without changing how many rows are returned.

What happens if you SELECT a column that isn't in the GROUP BY clause or an aggregate function? Most databases will throw an error, since it's ambiguous which row's value should represent the group. MySQL historically allowed this silently (picking an arbitrary row), which is a common source of subtle bugs.

content.text: Subqueries and Intermediate Concepts

items: What is a subquery? A query nested inside another query, often used in WHERE or FROM clauses to filter or compute intermediate results before the outer query runs.

What is the difference between a subquery and a JOIN? A JOIN combines columns from multiple tables into one result set; a subquery typically returns a single value or list used to filter another query. JOINs are often faster for large datasets since query optimizers generally handle them more efficiently than nested subqueries.

What is a Common Table Expression (CTE)? A named temporary result set defined with WITH, used to make complex queries more readable by breaking them into logical steps, and reusable multiple times within the same query without rewriting the logic.

What is the difference between a correlated and a non-correlated subquery? A correlated subquery references a column from the outer query and effectively runs once per outer row, which can be slow on large tables; a non-correlated subquery runs independently and only once, regardless of how many outer rows there are.

Can you use a CTE to replace a subquery, and why would you? Yes, and it's usually preferred once a query gets nested more than one or two levels deep, since a chain of CTEs reads top-to-bottom like a series of steps, while deeply nested subqueries have to be read from the inside out.

content.text: Advanced: Window Functions and Performance

items: What is a window function? A calculation across a set of related rows (a 'window') without collapsing them into a single output row, unlike GROUP BY. Common examples: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and SUM() OVER(PARTITION BY ...).

What is the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()? RANK() skips numbers after a tie (1,2,2,4); DENSE_RANK() does not (1,2,2,3). ROW_NUMBER() differs from both by never producing ties at all, assigning a unique sequential number even to identical values.

How would you find the second-highest salary in a table? Use LIMIT/OFFSET after ordering descending, a subquery with MAX() excluding the top value, or DENSE_RANK() filtered to rank 2, the DENSE_RANK() approach is usually preferred since it handles ties correctly and generalizes easily to 'Nth highest' by changing one number.

What is an index and why does it matter? An index is a data structure that speeds up row lookups on specific columns, at the cost of slightly slower writes and extra storage. Indexes matter most on columns frequently used in WHERE clauses or JOIN conditions on large tables.

What is normalization? The process of structuring tables to reduce data redundancy, typically by splitting data into related tables connected by keys. Denormalization (the opposite) sometimes happens deliberately in reporting/analytics databases to improve read performance, trading some redundancy for faster queries.

content.text: How to Actually Approach These in an Interview

In an interview, walking through your reasoning out loud, why a LEFT JOIN instead of INNER, why a CTE instead of a nested subquery, matters more than getting the syntax perfect on the first try. Interviewers are often more interested in whether you can explain a tradeoff (JOIN versus subquery performance, RANK versus DENSE_RANK behavior on ties) than whether you memorized exact syntax. If given a whiteboard or shared screen, writing the query incrementally, starting with a simple SELECT and adding joins/filters one at a time while narrating the logic, tends to leave a better impression than trying to write the full complex query in one pass.
Practicing Beyond This List

Reading through answers is a reasonable starting point, but the real skill only builds by writing queries against an actual dataset. A free public dataset (sales data, a movie database, anything with several related tables) loaded into a local SQLite or PostgreSQL instance is enough to practice every concept on this list hands-on, which builds far more confidence than reading explanations alone.

blogPosts.title: Data Analyst Roadmap: Skills, Tools, and Learning Path
blogPosts.summary: A structured path from zero to job-ready data analyst: which skills to learn first, which tools matter, and how to build a portfolio that gets interviews.

content.text: There are a lot of overlapping tools in the data analytics space, and it's easy to spend months learning things in the wrong order, or worse, learning five tools shallowly instead of two or three deeply enough to actually use on the job. This is the sequence that gets someone from beginner to job-ready the fastest, based on what actually gets used day-to-day in analyst roles.
Step 1: Excel (2-3 Weeks)

Excel is still the most commonly used analytics tool in real companies, even at organizations that also use Power BI or Python. Focus on PivotTables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, basic charting, conditional formatting, and simple one-page dashboards before moving on. This step also builds the spreadsheet-thinking mindset, rows, columns, aggregation, filtering, that makes SQL and Power BI dramatically easier to pick up later, since the underlying logic (group, filter, summarize) is the same across all three, just expressed differently.
Step 2: SQL (3-4 Weeks)

SQL is non-negotiable for almost every data analyst role, it's usually the first technical skill screened in an interview. Learn SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY first, then JOINs (inner, left, and self joins), then subqueries and CTEs, then window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, running totals). Practicing on a real dataset, even a free one, matters more than reading theory; SQL is a skill that only really sinks in through writing and debugging actual queries.
Step 3: Data Visualization — Power BI or Tableau (4-6 Weeks)

Pick one BI tool and go deep rather than learning both shallowly. Power BI is generally the more in-demand choice across most job markets right now, especially in organizations already using Microsoft's ecosystem (Excel, SharePoint, Teams). Learn data modeling (star schema, relationships), DAX basics (CALCULATE, SUMX, time-intelligence functions), and how to design a dashboard around business KPIs, not just how to make individual charts look nice.
Step 4: Python or R for Analysis (4-6 Weeks)

Python with Pandas and NumPy is the more common choice for analyst roles compared to R, mainly because Python's ecosystem extends more naturally into automation, APIs, and light machine learning if a role ever needs it. Focus on data cleaning, exploratory data analysis (EDA) with Seaborn or Matplotlib, and basic aggregation logic that mirrors what you already learned in Excel and SQL. Machine learning is optional at this stage, most analyst roles need clean, reliable analysis far more often than they need predictive models.
Step 5: Statistics Fundamentals (2-3 Weeks)

Mean, median, standard deviation, correlation versus causation, and basic hypothesis testing come up constantly, both in interviews and in real reporting work where someone will eventually ask 'is this difference actually significant, or just noise.' This doesn't need to be a full university-level statistics course, just enough to reason correctly about variance, sample size, and the difference between a trend and a coincidence.
Step 6: Build 3-4 Real Portfolio Projects (Ongoing, Alongside Steps 1-5)

Recruiters want to see a project that tells a business story: a problem, the approach taken, and a measurable outcome, not just a chart with no context. A sales dashboard, an HR or churn analysis tool, and one end-to-end SQL-to-BI pipeline project (extract data with SQL, model and visualize in Power BI) cover most of what recruiters look for across a typical analyst job description. Building these gradually while learning each tool, rather than waiting until 'everything' is learned first, keeps the whole process from feeling abstract.
Step 7: Apply, Get Feedback, Iterate

Start applying before every skill feels 'complete', no one feels fully ready, and waiting for that feeling delays real progress. Real interview feedback, the specific questions that stump you, the gaps that show up under pressure, is a faster teacher than more tutorials, since it points directly at what to study next instead of guessing. Keep refining the portfolio and resume based on what actually comes up in interviews, not based on generic advice alone.
A Realistic Weekly Structure

For someone studying alongside a job or other commitments, a workable split is roughly: two focused sessions a week on the current tool being learned, one session applying it to a portfolio project, and one session on SQL practice specifically, since SQL fluency degrades faster than other skills without regular use and gets tested in nearly every interview. Consistency across weeks matters far more than occasional long study marathons.
Realistic Timeline

For someone studying consistently, four to six months covers the full path from beginner to job-ready, faster with prior technical background (especially if coming from a related field like finance, engineering, or a numerate degree), slower if learning part-time alongside other commitments. Consistency matters more than speed, three focused hours a week for six months outperforms an intense two-week binge followed by months of no practice.
What Happens After Landing the First Role

The learning doesn't stop at the first job, most analysts keep building depth in whichever tool their company relies on most heavily, and start picking up adjacent skills like stakeholder communication, requirements gathering, and eventually some exposure to data engineering concepts (ETL pipelines, data warehousing) as they move toward more senior analyst or analytics engineering roles. The roadmap above gets someone in the door; what happens after depends heavily on the specific company and team.
Skills That Get Overlooked in Most Roadmaps

Beyond the technical tools, two skills quietly separate strong analysts from average ones and rarely show up in roadmap checklists: asking good clarifying questions before starting an analysis, and communicating a finding in plain language to someone non-technical. A technically perfect analysis that answers the wrong question, because the requirements were never clarified, wastes as much time as not knowing SQL at all. Similarly, a great insight buried in jargon-heavy language often gets ignored simply because the person reading it can't quickly extract the actionable part.

These skills develop mostly through practice, presenting findings out loud (even to a friend or in a mock interview setting), and getting comfortable rephrasing a technical result into a single, plain-language sentence a manager could act on without needing any background in the tools used to get there.

testimonials.name: Analytics Mentor
testimonials.quote: Krishna shows strong curiosity, clear dashboard thinking, and a practical approach to turning data into insights.

testimonials.name: Team Collaborator
testimonials.quote: He communicates well, learns quickly, and contributes with ownership across analytics and web technology tasks.

testimonials.name: Academic Guide
testimonials.quote: His project work reflects a solid foundation in Python, visualization, and applied machine learning concepts.

services.title: Dashboard Development
services.detail: Power BI, Excel, and Tableau dashboards for sales, operations, and KPI tracking.

services.title: Data Analysis Support
services.detail: Python, SQL, and spreadsheet analysis for reports, trends, insights, and decision support.

services.title: Machine Learning Prototype
services.detail: Entry-level ML models, experiments, visualizations, and model performance summaries.

faqs.question: What services can Krishna help with?
faqs.answer: Dashboards, SQL/Python analysis, KPI reporting, data cleaning, and beginner ML prototypes.

faqs.question: How can recruiters contact Krishna?
faqs.answer: Use the contact form, WhatsApp CTA, email button, LinkedIn, or appointment request form.

faqs.question: Is the contact form connected to email?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Netlify function forwards contact and lead messages to Krishna by email.
